figures.caption: Fig. 12. Failure cases. The left example shows errors caused by noise in the scanned garment mesh, while the right example highlights limitations in the design of the current synthetic data.

Fig. 11. Qualitative comparisons on the application of single-view reconstruction. From left to right: (a) the input image, (b) the generated 3D clothed human mesh, (c)-(d) the draping result with corresponding BoxMesh and 2D sewing pattern of AIpparel [15], ChatGarment [16], and ours-SVR, respectively.

Fig. 1. Examples of sewing pattern reconstruction from 3D garments obtained via multi-view capture with smartphones (left) and single-view reconstruction (right). Each triplet shows the input, the “scanned” clothed human from the input, and our predicted sewing pattern.

Fig. 5. Evaluation on Stage II. From left to right: (a)-(c) the draped 3D garment with sewing pattern displayed in 2D and the corresponding BoxMesh of ground truth, Ours-GT* and Ours-Pred*, respectively; (d) the input BoxMesh predicted from Stage I.
TABLE I EVALUATION ON GARMENT-TO-BOXMESH ON GCD TESTING SET.

Fig. 9. Ablation study with Inter Points. From left to right: (a) the input garment mesh, (b)-(c) the draped 3D garment, the corresponding BoxMesh, and the input points of Stage II from Inter Points and Full, (d) ground truth.

Fig. 13. More results of our method. From left to right: (a) input 3D scan (the first row is from RenderPeople and the second to fourth rows are from THuman) or single-view image (last two rows), (b) segmented 3D garment with fitted body or reconstructed 3D clothed human from single view, (c) 3D garment deformed into A-pose, (d) our result after simulation.
